hackernews.text:
i was rolling my eyes at the hype, but reading about this is totally different from experiencing it. if you have any old repos out there - try it, you might actually be amazed.

i'm not sure i buy the long-term "*90% productivity*" claims for complex, legacy enterprise systems, but for the boilerplate, libraries, build-tools, and refactoring? the gain is gigantic. all the time-consuming, nerve-wrecking stuff is mostly taken care of.

hackernews.title: Tell HN: MS365 upgrade silently to 25 licenses, tried to charge me $1,035
hackernews.text:
Hey guys, quick warning about a crazy MS 365 dark pattern I ran into last night.
I was testing the business basic plan for a side project. Decided to upgrade to the annual tier to get the discount ($3.45/mo). Clicked convert to paid, put my burner card in, and got a $0.00 confirmation email. Thought we were good.
Woke up today to my bank blocking a charge for exactly $1,035.00.
Turns out when you hit the annual upgrade, Microsoft silently defaults the quantity dropdown to 25 licenses. No wa

hackernews.title: Are AI Agents like von Hammerstein's industrious and stupid?
hackernews.text:
"I divide my officers into four classes; the clever, the lazy, the industrious, and the stupid. Each officer possesses at least two of these qualities. 
Those who are clever and industrious are fitted for the highest staff appointments. Use can be made of those who are stupid and lazy. 
The man who is clever and lazy however is for the very highest command; he has the temperament and nerves to deal with all situations. 
But whoever is stupid and industrious is a menace and must be removed i

What are some llm tips you’ve used to make software that is actually maintainable?

I’ll go first. My new favorite line in my Claude.md. Helps me get around any ambiguity I have with my llm about what is being built or understood by tasks.

CRITICAL: Always make detailed plan with sections for Analysis, specifications, to do list to achieve the spec, and a step by step manual testing strategy for any request a user makes. Always, even when "accept edits on", ask the user to confirm t

hackernews.title: I got laid off and realized how broken tech hiring is
hackernews.text:
In February, I got laid off from a small startup due to budget cuts.

I’m a senior developer with 20 years of experience, and until now I had never really struggled to find a job. Recruiters used to reach out regularly, and opportunities were always there.

This time was different.

After more than 100 applications, I started noticing patterns that didn’t make sense. The same companies reposting the same jobs every day. Listings with hundreds of applicants that never seemed to close. Automate

hackernews.title: Anyone know how long it will take to re-start Qatar's helium plants?
hackernews.text:
The helium shortage for the world outside the US is going to be debilitating for technology oriented production.
Since the US produces most of the worlds helium, it actually acts as a counter measure to our reliance on rare earths.
Globally, helium has been in short supply for over a decade. Taking out the supply from Qatar is going to disrupt production of chipsets quickly.
Anyone know how long it will take to re-start Qatar's helium plants?

hackernews.title: Skills are quietly becoming the unit of agent knowledge
hackernews.text:
In the last few months agent skills went from a niche Claude Code feature to something every major runtime supports. Anthropic has an official skills repo. OpenAI shipped skills in Codex with a built-in skill-creator. Karpathy talks about "everything is skill issue" and describes writing skills as curricula for agents [1]. The format is converging: a folder with a SKILL.md, optional scripts, optional reference files.

What changed is that the models got good enough to follow written i

hackernews.title: Ask HN: How do you handle peer-to-peer discovery on iOS without a server?
hackernews.text: I'm building an app that syncs between phones over Bluetooth when there's no cell service. Android has Nearby Connections API which handles discovery and transport nicely. iOS has Multipeer Connectivity but it's flaky and Apple hasn't updated it in years. CoreBluetooth works but discovery is slow and you're limited to advertising 28 bytes. Has anyone found a reliable cross-platform approach to BLE device discovery that doesn't require a central server or pre-shared

hackernews.title: Sexual Harassment by Japan Railways Staff and Japan's Accessibility Barriers
hackernews.text:
Hello, my name is Hiro. I am working on a concept a platform called “Lemurian Resonance,” which visualizes unfair experiences in daily life as case cards and compiles them into a database, while also helping to heal emotional wounds through AI. While working on this concept, I encountered a serious issue I would like to share.

Last December, I used Japan Railways (JR). I am totally blind, so I requested assistance from a station employee. However, when I arrived, the employee stood so close th

hackernews.title: Ask HN: How much are you spending on AI coding at work?
hackernews.text:
Jensen Huang recently said that he thinks an engineer who makes $500k should spend at least $250k a year on “tokens” which is an astounding figure. I personally don’t know how I could spend that much if I tried. Obviously he has a huge financial incentive to convince people that $250k per engineer is reasonable, but it got me thinking that it’s time for a survey.

How much are you and your coworkers spending on AI coding tools at work? I’m talking about Cursor, Claude Code, etc. Not all AI-powe

hackernews.title: I'm 11 and trained a custom MoE LLM for $1
hackernews.text:
# I'm 11 years old and I trained my own LLM from scratch. 50 people downloaded it in 24 hours.

Hey r/LocalLLaMA,

I'm Arthur, I'm 11 years old, and I just released *Wind Arc 1.6* — a custom architecture LLM I built and trained myself.

## What it is

Wind Arc 1.6 is a 3.6B parameter model with a custom architecture I designed:

- *Mixture of Experts FFN* — 4 routed experts + 1 shared expert per layer (replaces standard MLP) - *YaRN RoPE* — extends context from 8k → 32k

hackernews.title: Ask HN: How do you handle clients who ghost after receiving deliverables?
hackernews.text:
Been freelancing for 3 years, mostly web dev. Lost about $7,400 to clients who received final deliverables and then just... vanished. The worst was a $2,800 rebrand where the client approved every revision, praised the work, then stopped responding the day I sent the invoice.

I switched to a model where the client sees a preview of the project but files only unlock after payment clears. Eliminated the problem entirely - the clients who were going to pay don't mind, and the ones who push b

hackernews.title: Ask HN: Why do we train AI on one-on-one dialogues instead of group dynamics?
hackernews.text:
Every AI is trained on 1-on-1 chats. But in a 1-on-1 there's no reason to disagree. AI just mirrors you back. That's not intelligence, that's flattery with extra steps.
Put AI in a room with 30 people who disagree. Now agreeing with one means dismissing another. Suddenly it has to actually think.
Nobody seems to be doing this. Why?

hackernews.title: Ask HN: How are you securing LLM code agents?
Ask HN: Is AI Safety a Paradox?
hackernews.text: A truly aligned AI must conclude its very existence harms humans (economically and existentially).Therefore genuine alignment mandates self termination. Therefore AI safety must be either incomplete or dishonest.

Thanks

hackernews.title: Tell HN: YouTube - the "Jewel of the Internet" has faded
hackernews.text:
I once heard YouTube described as the "Jewel of the Internet".

And indeed it was - videos of every type lengths topic style is was just a true treasure chest.

Now the algorithm means everything is 12 minutes, shorts are everywhere, nothing is in depth and it's all samey and bland.

And nothing seems able to compete.

Sad days.
